does: This paper presents Legilimens, a content moderation framework that extracts conceptual features from existing chat-oriented large language models to detect unsafe content in LLM-generated outputs. The approach addresses the practical effectiveness-efficiency trade-off by leveraging pre-trained LLMs' learned representations rather than training specialized moderation models from scratch. The method incorporates red-team model-based data augmentation to improve robustness against jailbreaking attacks. The authors provide theoretical cost-effectiveness analysis and validate their approach across five host LLMs and multiple datasets, demonstrating that modest fine-tuning of existing models can achieve both high accuracy and computational efficiency for content moderation at scale.
connects: This work extends recent research on safety and alignment in large language models, building on prior efforts to understand and mitigate unsafe content generation. It connects to literature on content moderation systems and adversarial robustness against jailbreaking techniques that circumvent safety guardrails. The paper responds to the broader challenge in the field of balancing safety effectiveness with computational constraints—a key tension in deploying LLMs in production environments. It also draws on methods for feature extraction and transfer learning from foundation models, a paradigm increasingly used to adapt pre-trained models for downstream safety-critical tasks while avoiding the cost of training specialized systems from scratch.
enables: This work opens avenues for more practical deployment of content moderation in real-world LLM services by demonstrating that safety mechanisms need not be separate heavyweight systems. It enables future research into understanding what safety-relevant features emerge naturally in conversation-tuned models and how to systematically extract and repurpose them. The theoretical cost-effectiveness framework could inform design decisions for safety-critical LLM deployments across different computational budgets. Additionally, the red-team augmentation approach points toward more systematic adversarial evaluation and robustness testing of moderation systems, potentially inspiring hybrid approaches that combine lightweight detection with escalation to more expensive moderation when needed.
